subtopic: Bhagavad Gita Features and Natyashastra Basics
title: Bhagavad Gita Features and Natyashastra Basics
focus: Discusses the synthesis of traditions in the Gita and introduces the Natyashastra as the epitome of performing arts.
rationale: Fallback section derived from packet-level grouping.
sectionExcerpt:
[Page 031]
About Bhagavad-Gita
* Attributed to Sage Vyas, it contains 700 verses in 18 chapters, and is embedded within the Bhishmaparva (adhyaya 23-40) of the epic Mahabharata.
* It is considered to be composed in the 2nd or 1st century BCE.
* Synthesizes Vedic, Buddhist, Jain, and Carvaka thought traditions.
* Presented as a dialogue between Krishna and Arjuna on the battlefield of Kurukshetra, aimed at dispelling Arjuna's dejection (vishada).
* Krishna urges Arjuna to perform his duty without attachment to the results (nishkama karma).
* Proposes three main paths (margas): Karma (action), Jnana (knowledge), and Bhakti (devotion).
* Key features:
* Introduces the concept of Bhakti.
* Inspired the teachings of Sankaradeva, founder of the Vaishnavite sect in Assam.
About Natyashastra (Science of Dramaturgy)

[Page 032]
Nāṭyaśāstra of Bharatamuni. • Codified around the 2nd century B.C., the Nāṭyaśāstra of Bharatamuni is an epitome of the Nāṭyaveda, an oral body of knowledge of performing art containing 36,000 verses known as the Gāndharvaveda. • The Nāṭyaśāstra embodies a comprehensive set of rules that define nāṭya (drama), abhinaya (performance), rasa (aesthetic experience), bhāva (emotion), saṅgīta (music), among others, thus articulating the character of Indian theatre, poetics, aesthetics, dance and music.
